coredata.dc:title: An “on-off-on” fluorescent nanoprobe for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on phosphorus/nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dot
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Xiaojuan
Liu, Yang
Yang, Zhenhua
Shuang, Shaomin
Zhang, Zeyu
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: Chromium (VI) [Cr(VI)] is a harsh environmental contaminates and has been proved to be highly toxic, carcinogenic and mutagenic. Therefore, developing an inexpensive, good selective and highly sensitive nanoprobe for the detection of Cr(VI) is in urgent demand. Recently, the highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been successfully utilized as efficient fluorescent nanoprobes for the detection of ions, pH and molecular substances. In this work, an “on-off” fluorescence phosphorus/nitrogen dual-doped CQDs (PNCQDs) probe was developed for the determination of Cr(VI) based on inner filter effect (IFE). The proposed PNCQDs nanoprobe shows its distinct merits of simplicity, convenience, fast implementation, good selectivity and high sensitivity towards Cr(VI), allowing its potential application in the determination of Cr(VI) in environment and biosystem. In addition, the chelation effect of the functional groups in reductant and Cr(VI), and the easy-conversion of Cr(VI) to reduced states (i.e. Cr(III) and Cr(0)) by reductants makes the minimization of IFE with a concomitant recovery of PNCQDs fluorescence possible. Hence, the PNCQDs/Cr(VI) hybrid was used as an “off-on” fluorescence probe for sensing ascorbic acid (AA), which is a model reductant. For the detection of Cr(VI), the linear range and the limit of detection achieved were 1.5–30 μmol/L and 23 nmol/L, respectively. For the detection of AA, the linear range and the limit of detection obtained were 5.0–200 μmol/L and 1.35 μmol/L, respectively. The as-constructed “on-off-on” PNCQDs fluorescent nanoprobe was successfully applied for detecting Cr(VI) and AA in biosystem. Furthermore, the as-constructed fluorescent sensing system was successfully applied to the analyses of AA in fresh fruits and in commercial fruit juices with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phosphorus/nitrogen dual-doped
Carbon quantum dot nanoprobe
Chromium(VI)
Ascorbic acid
Inner filter effect

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of phosphorus/nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dots (PNCQDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and (B) particle size distribution of the as-fabricated PNCQDs. (C) C 1s XPS, (D) N 1s XPS, and (E) P 2p XPS of the as-fabricated PNCQDs.

(A) UV−vis absorption (black line), PL excitation (red line), and emission (blue line) spectra of PNCQDs in aqueous solution (0.10 mg/mL). The inset shows photographs of PNCQDs aqueous solution (0.30 mg/mL) under visible (left) and UV light (right). (B) Excitation-dependent PL behavior of PNCQDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence quenching of PNCQDs in the presence of Cr(VI) (0.0–150 μmol/L). The inset at the top right is the SV plot of PNCQDs at different concentrations of Cr(VI) (1.5–30 μmol/L) where F
0 /F are fluorescence intensities of PNCQDs (0.20 mg/mL) without and with Cr(VI), respectively. (B) The overlap between absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) and excitation and emission spectra of PNCQDs. (C) Fluorescence decay of PNCQDs (0.10 mg/mL) in the absence and presence of Cr(VI) (0.50 mmol/L) as a function of time at λ ex/λ em of 405/502 nm.

Fluorescence recovery of the PNCQDs/Cr(VI) mixture to AA at various concentrations (0.0–400 μmol/L). The inset at the top right is the plot of fluorescent intensity of PNCQDs/Cr(VI) mixture against AA concentration in the range of 5.0–200 μmol/L.
Cell viability test of PNCQDs on SiHa cells. The values represent percentage cell viability (mean% ± SD, n = 6).

(A) LSCM images of SiHa cells incubated with (i) 0.30 mg/mL PNCQDs at 37 °C for 2 h, (ii) different concentration of Cr(VI) ((a) 0.25 mmol/L, (b) 0.50 mmol/L, (c) 0.75 mmol/L, and (d) 1.0 mmol/L) added into nutrient solutions and incubated with SiHa cells at room temperature for 2.0 min, (iii) different concentration of AA ((a) 2.5 mmol/L, (b) 5.0 mmol/L, and (c) 10 mmol/L) added into nutrient solutions and incubated with SiHa cells at room temperature for 3.0 min, respectively. The first and second panels are cell images recorded on λ ex/λ em of 488/500 ± 25 and 543/650 ± 25 nm, respectively. The third panels are the merged images of the first and second panels. (B) Intracellular fluorescence intensities of PNCQDs, PNCQDs/Cr(VI) (1.0 mmol/L), and PNCQDs/Cr(VI) (1.0 mol/L)/AA (10 mmol/L). Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 6. The emission intensities of 21 cells were monitored. λ ex/λ em are 488/500 ± 25 nm. Scale bar, 20 μm.
Diagrammatical explanation of fluorescence mechanism for Cr(VI) and AA based on IFE of PNCQDs.
Recovery of AA in fresh fruits.
Recovery of AA in commercial fruit juices.

head.ce:title: An “on-off-on” fluorescent nanoprobe for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on phosphorus/nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dot

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Science, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chromium (VI) [Cr(VI)] is a harsh environmental contaminates and has been proved to be highly toxic, carcinogenic and mutagenic. Therefore, developing an inexpensive, good selective and highly sensitive nanoprobe for the detection of Cr(VI) is in urgent demand. Recently, the highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been successfully utilized as efficient fluorescent nanoprobes for the detection of ions, pH and molecular substances. In this work, an “on-off” fluorescence phosphorus/nitrogen dual-doped CQDs (PNCQDs) probe was developed for the determination of Cr(VI) based on inner filter effect (IFE). The proposed PNCQDs nanoprobe shows its distinct merits of simplicity, convenience, fast implementation, good selectivity and high sensitivity towards Cr(VI), allowing its potential application in the determination of Cr(VI) in environment and biosystem. In addition, the chelation effect of the functional groups in reductant and Cr(VI), and the easy-conversion of Cr(VI) to reduced states (i.e. Cr(III) and Cr(0)) by reductants makes the minimization of IFE with a concomitant recovery of PNCQDs fluorescence possible. Hence, the PNCQDs/Cr(VI) hybrid was used as an “off-on” fluorescence probe for sensing ascorbic acid (AA), which is a model reductant. For the detection of Cr(VI), the linear range and the limit of detection achieved were 1.5–30 μmol/L and 23 nmol/L, respectively. For the detection of AA, the linear range and the limit of detection obtained were 5.0–200 μmol/L and 1.35 μmol/L, respectively. The as-constructed “on-off-on” PNCQDs fluorescent nanoprobe was successfully applied for detecting Cr(VI) and AA in biosystem. Furthermore, the as-constructed fluorescent sensing system was successfully applied to the analyses of AA in fresh fruits and in commercial fruit juices with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past few decades, heavy-metal ions were constantly released into the environment through industrial and other anthropogenic processes. Comparing to chromium(III) [Cr(III)] and/or Cr(0), Cr(VI) [1,2] is more risky to community hygiene owing to its significant migratory and cancerigenic characteristics. Consequently, Cr(VI) detection in environmental samples is of significant importance. According to the US Environmental Protection Agency, the maximum permissible Cr(VI) concentration in potable water is 0.1 mg/L [3]. To examine and control potable water quality and to take the edge off the risks of released salvaged materials, the analytical method possessing good selectivity, high sensitivity and rapid response is in urgent demand in order to realize the Cr(VI) determination at low concentration levels. Numerous analytical techniques have been successfully developed for Cr(VI) detection in different sample matrices, including chromatography [4], spectrofluorimetry [5], atomic absorption spectroscopy [6] and so on. Nevertheless, most of the reported detection methods require expensive equipment, complicated sample pretreatment and inferior selectivity and sensitivity. As such, a simple, good selective and high sensitive method Cr(VI) detection is highly necessary. Due to the unique advantages of fluorescence nanoprobes in good selectivity, high sensitivity and facile manipulation, the nanoprobes based on the changes in fluorescence intensity for the detection of Cr(VI) come into existence as the situation requires. Recently, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-based fluorescence nanoprobes for Cr(VI) detection have received much attention. Although there are several reports on the successful application of CQDs-based nanoprobes for Cr(VI) detection [5,7], the achievements in these area are comparatively few.

The ascorbic acid (2-(1,2-dihydroxyethyl)-4,5-dihydroxyfuran-3-one, AA), which possesses antioxidant properties, is a kind of water-soluble vitamin C. AA exists widely in human diet, pharmaceutical formulation and cosmetic products [8]. Besides its popular antioxidant properties, AA was widely directed at prophylaxis and therapy of rheum, scurvy, spiritual illnesses, cancer, cardiovascular disease and acquired immune deficiency syndrome [9]. To this end, the determination of AA content in pharmaceutical preparations and food products has received considerable attention, and a straightforward and effective method for AA detection in daily analysis is urgent to be developed. To date, numerous methods for AA detection have been developed, including titration with an oxidizing agent [10], electrochemistry [11], spectrophotometry [12], chromatography [13], chemiluminescence [5,14], enzymology [15] and capillary electrophoresis [16]. Nevertheless, more simple, effective and sensitive methods for AA detection in food samples are still in urgent demand.

The fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have recently attracted growing interests due to their distinct benefits in low cost, robust chemical inertness, over simplified fabrication path, small size, strong photochemical stability, good solubility, tunable excitation/emission, better biocompatibility and lower cytotoxicity [5,8,17–19]. On the basis of these outstanding properties, the as-constructed CQDs-based fluorescence nanoprobes were successfully used for the determination of pH [19], ions (K+ [20], Ag+ [21], Cu2+ [22], Hg2+ [23], Co2+ [24], Pb2+ [25], Fe3+ [26], PO4 3− [27], etc.) and molecular substances (DNA [28], nitrite [29], glucose [30], biothiol [31], curcumin [32], etc.) by measuring the variations in the fluorescence intensities.

The classical probing theories include intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), photo-induced electron transfer (PET), twisted intramolecular charge transfer (TICT), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), metal-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) and electronic energy transfer (EET) [5,33].

Generally speaking, the probing systems ground on the above theories concern the intermolecular mutual effect between the target molecule and the probe, which just operate when they are at an extraordinary interval or have a supplementary geometry, resulting in these methods being sophisticated and time consuming, consequently, further limiting the actual application. A selective path to devise fluorescence experiments is on account of inner filter effect (IFE), which is known to arise from the absorption of the excited and/or emitted light by absorbers in the detection system [34,35]. Comparing to other mechanisms, this method could achieve an intensive sensitivity since the changes in the absorbance of probes transform exponentially into fluorescence intensity changes. Nevertheless, the CQDs-based nanoprobe by fluorescent IFE was rarely explored.

In this work, the phosphorus and nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dots (PNCQDs) with bright green photoluminescence (PL) were conveniently synthesized by mixing glucose, 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) and concentrated phosphoric acid (H3PO4). The PNCQDs serve as IFE-based fluorescence nanoprobes for Cr(VI) detection based on the remarkable fluorescence quenching mechanism (on-off, Scheme 1 ). The proposed PNCQDs sensing system possess numerous superiorities in moderate qualification (normal temperature, neutral environment), rapider implementation (in 30 s) and higher sensitivity (K
SV = 1.15 × 105 L/mol, LOD = 23 nmol/L) as compared to other recorded techniques. Furthermore, the PNCQDs/Cr(VI) mixture could act as a “turn-on” fluorescence nanoprobe (off-on, Scheme 1) for the determination of AA. Comparing to other AA probe [5,12–14,16], it possess high sensitivity (LOD = 1.35 μmol/L), low cost, high selectivity and fast response (within 1 min).

Glucose, 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA), and concentrated phosphoric acid (H3PO4) were purchased from Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI, USA). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), trypsin, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) were obtained from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Other reagents were bought from Beijing Chemical Reagents Company (Beijing, China). All reagents are analytical grade or above, which were used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water (≥18.25 MΩ·cm) from a Milli-Q Plus System (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) were used to prepare all aqueous solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of phosphorus/nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dots (PNCQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The homogeneous solutions of EDA (6.0 mL) and concentrated H3PO4 (4.0 mL) were quickly mixed with 0.40 g glucose in a 100 mL beaker without stirring. The reaction mixture was quickly foamed by the acid-base neutralization spontaneous heat. It was then gradually cooled down to room temperature within 6 min. The crude product was dialyzed against ultrapure water for 72 h through a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut-off, MWCO: 500–1000 Da) and then filtered. Finally, PNCQDs in dark brown colour were collected and lyophilized to obtain the dry PNCQDs product.

The transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were obtained on a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (Tokyo, Japan). The atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were acquired on a Veeco Nanoscope Quadrex AFM (Bruker AFM Probes, Camarillo, CA, U.S.A.). Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were performed on a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, Worcestershire, U.K.). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded on a Kratos AXIS ULTRA DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were acquired on a Bruker Tensor II FTIR spectrometer (Bremen, Germany). The UV-vis and photoluminescence spectra were performed on a Lambda 950 absorption spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Llantrisant, UK) and Varian Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer (Palo Alto, CA, USA), respectively. Nanosecond fluorescence lifetime assays were recorded by FLS 920 time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) system (Edinburgh Instruments, England).

The quantum yield (Φ
S ) of as-fabricated PNCQDs sample was determined by comparing the integrated photoluminescence (PL) intensities and optical density (OD) of the as-fabricated PNCQDs with the reference quinine sulfate (Φ
R = 0.54) in 0.10 mol/L H2SO4 (refractive index, η = 1.33) while the as-fabricated PNCQDs was dissolved in distilled water (η = 1.33) at different concentrations. A Lambda 950 absorption spectrophotometer was used to determine the OD (absorbance) of the solutions at 380 nm. A Varian Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer was used to record their PL spectra with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The integrated PL intensity was the area under the PL curve in the wavelength range of 405–700 nm. Then a graph of integrated PL intensity against absorbance was plotted. The Φ
S of the as-fabricated PNCQDs were calculated using equation (1):
(1)
Φ x
=
Φ
s t
(
K x
/
K
s t
)
(
η x
/
η
s t
)
2
where K is the gradient from the plot of integrated PL intensity against OD and η is the refractive index. The subscript “st” and “x” refer to the standard and sample, respectively. For these aqueous solutions, η
x /η
st = 1. In order to minimise the self-absorption effect, the OD was kept under 0.10 at the excitation wavelength in the 10-mm path-length fluorescence cuvette [36]. Fig. S1 depicted the calibration curves of quinine sulfate and PNCQDs.

The concentration of PNCQDs in PBS solution (pH 7.4) was 0.20 mg/mL. Then, the aqueous solutions containing nineteen kinds of metal ions(K+, Na+, Ag+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+ and Cr(VI)) and fifteen kinds of anion ions(F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, NO2 −, H2PO4 −, SCN−, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, S2O3 2−, HPO4 2−, SO3 2−, C2O4 2− and Cr2O7 2−) were prepared in a concentration of 0.10 mol/L, respectively. To evaluate the selectivity of PNCQDs, each 10 μL of the above ion solution was mingled with 2.0 mL PNCQDs, and the PL intensities were measured. To evaluate the interferences of different ions, each 0.5 mL of the test ions solution was mixed with 1.5 mL PNCQDs, then the PL intensities and spectra were estimated. Subsequently, 5.0 μL Cr(VI) was added into the above mixture solution, and then the PL intensities and spectra were measured. To access the linear range and the detection limit of Cr(VI), 0.20 mL of the solutions containing different concentrations of Cr(VI) was mixed with 1.8 mL PNCQDs. The sample obtained by mixing 0.20 mL of ultrapure water with 1.80 mL PNCQDs was used as a control sample. All the samples were excited (λ ex) at 403 nm, and the fluorescence emission (λ em) were recorded at 502 nm.

In a typical procedure, 10 μL of the Cr(VI) (0.1 mol/L) was added into 1.93 mL of PNCQDs solution (0.20 mg/mL) in order to form the probe (PNCQDs/Cr(VI)). Then, 60 μL of AA was added, and the mixture was shaken thoroughly at room temperature. After 3 min, the fluorescence recovery spectra were measured under the λ ex/λ em at 403/502 nm. The selectivity of the PNCQDs/Cr(VI) probe towards AA was confirmed by adding other reducing agents (Na2S, NaNO2, Na2SO3, KI, KBr, Na2S2O3, glucose, GSH, Glutamic acid, Glycine, Citric acid and Ascorbic acid) instead of AA in a similar way. To evaluate the linear range and the detection limit of AA, 10 μL of an AA solution at various concentrations (0.10 mmol/L - 0.10 mol/L) was then added and measured following the same processing method described above. For the fluorescence analysis of AA in real samples, 10 μL of a sample solution was added into 1.99 mL probe (PNCQDs/Cr(VI)) solution, and they were mixed thoroughly at room temperature. After 3 min, fluorescence recovery spectra were recorded under the λ ex/λ em at 403/502 nm. The content of AA in real samples was calculated according to a linear equation obtained from a standard solution of AA.
Human cervical carcinoma SiHa cell was chosen to test the cell cytotoxicity of PNCQDs. The experimental procedures were used according to our previously published papers [19,37].

The SiHa cells were incubated in DMEM supplied with 10% FBS, and the cells were adhered on the culture dish and incubated with 0.30 mg/mL PNCQDs, 0.30 mg/mL PNCQDs/Cr(VI) (0.25 mmol/L, 0.50 mmol/L, 0.75 mmol/L and 1.0 mmol/L) and 0.30 mg/mL PNCQDs/1.0 mmol/L Cr(VI)/AA (2.5 mmol/L, 5.0 mmol/L and 10 mmol/L), respectively. After incubation at 37 °C for 2 h or several minutes, SiHa cells were harvested using 0.25% trypsin/0.02% EDTA, then rinsed three times (1.0 mL each) with PBS (pH 7.4) and kept in PBS for optical imaging by an Olympus FV1000 confocal microscope (Tokyo, Japan) with 20 × objectives.
All of the juices and fruits were bought from a local supermarket in Taiyuan, China, and used immediately after treatment.

Commercial fruit juices, i.e., orange juice, peach juice, and sea-buckthorn juice, were centrifuged at 10 °C in a refrigerated centrifuge at 8000 rpm for 5.0 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.45 μm pore size filter membrane and then diluted to an appropriate concentration in the detection range with ultrapure water.

Fresh fruits (orange, red grape and kiwi) samples were prepared according to the following approaches. First, all of the samples were washed with ultrapure water and dried naturally in air at room temperature. They were then accurately weighed and homogenized by using a commercial blender. After centrifugation at 10 °C in a refrigerated centrifuge at 3000 rpm for 20min, they underwent the same processing method used for the commercial fruit juices described above.

The PNCQDs were prepared by acid-base reaction heat method which was fully described in Fig. S2. In brief, 6.0 mL EDA was mixed with 0.40 g glucose and followed by the addition of 4.0 mL concentrated H3PO4 to produce PNCQDs. The yield of PNCQDs is approximately 6931%, which was shown in Table S1. Because of the foaming of the boiling glucose from the spontaneous heat (130 °C), the volume of the viscous slurry mixture was dramatically expanded. Within 30 s, the color of the mixture changed to nigger-brown. The reaction continued about 3 min until exhaustion of the acid-base neutralization heat. This spontaneous reaction is a cost-effective way to fabricate PNCQDs without external heating. The self-engendered nanobubbles were produced to remove the excessive heat and were used as the templates to limit the size of PNCQDs sample. The structure and the morphology of PNCQDs were measured by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 1 A depicted the characteristic TEM image of the as-fabricated PNCQDs. It can be seen that the PNCQDs are of primary orbicular morphology and spread around evenly on the copper grid surface. A relevant histogram which was acquired by statistical analysis of approximately 100 particles using the ImageJ software, was displayed in Fig. 1B. The Gaussian fitting curves show that PNCQDs possess a narrow size distribution of 4.24–6.33 nm with an average diameter of 5.14 ± 0.2 nm. The AFM image of PNCQDs was depicted in Fig. S3, demonstrating that PNCQDs possess a narrow size distribution with a mean height of ca. 3.8 nm. The DLS result (Fig. S4) indicates that the average hydrodynamic particle size of PNCQDs is 10.15 ± 0.60 nm, revealing that PNCQDs are stable without any aggregation in ultrapure water solution. This result is consistent with the TEM analysis results, whereas DLS estimates the whole hydrodynamic particle size.

To investigate the chemical composition of the as-fabricated PNCQDs, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. S5) and the elemental analysis (Table S2) were performed. As can be seen from Fig. S5A, the XPS spectrum of PNCQDs exhibits five peaks at 131.3, 191.5, 283.4, 397.7 and 528.6 eV, corresponding to P 2p, P 2s, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1C) was disassembled into five surface constituents, which are contributed to C 1s states in sp2 (CC), C-C/C-P, C-OH/C-O-C, CN/C-P and CO, corresponding to the binding energy of 284.29 eV, 284.87 eV, 285.52 eV, 286.1 eV and 287.32 eV, respectively [37–39]. The N 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1D) demonstrates four peaks at 397.85, 398.35, 399.0 and 399.83 eV, which are associated with C-N-C, N-(C)3, pyridinic N and pyrrolic N, respectively [37,38]. The high-resolution P 2p peak in Fig. 1E shows P 2p3/2 and P-C binding motifs of PNCQDs [37,38]. The O1s spectrum of the PNCQDs in Fig. S5B depicts four peaks at 529.02, 529.64, 530.22 and 530.88 eV, corresponding to O 1s states in O-C-O, PO, C-OH/C-O-C and P-O, respectively [37,38]. The elemental analysis results presented in Table S2A show that the PNCQDs was comprised of C 18.94, H 7.28, N 17.86, P 16.54 wt % and O (calculated) 39.38 wt %, indicating that they are phosphorus and nitrogen dual-doped CQDs. Table S2B describes the relative number of atoms which was expressed according to the elemental contents. The empirical formula for a PNCQD is approximately C6H27N5P2O9. Therefore, all data indicate that P and N have been successfully doped into the graphite structure of the PNCQDs.

As shown in Fig. S6, FTIR spectroscopy was used to characterize the functional groups of PNCQDs. A broad absorption peak at 3473 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C-OH, revealing that PNCQDs contain plenty of hydroxyl groups [39,40]. Three sharp peaks at 2858, 1567 and 1408 cm−1 are corresponding to the C-H stretching, CN stretching and CH3 umbrella bending, respectively [38,40]. The other characteristic peaks ascribed to phosphorus and/or oxygen-containing groups, such as P-O-H (960 cm−1), P-O-C(aromatic) (1075 cm−1), hydrogen-bonded PO (1148 cm−1), CO vibration (1639 cm−1) and P-OH bending (2500−2760 cm−1) are also observed [37,38,40]. The zeta potential (ζ) of PNCQDs (Fig. S7) is −9.3 mV. In essence, the abundant functional groups not only endow PNCQDs with negative charges, but also improve the hydrophilicity and stability of PNCQDs in aqueous system.

The optical properties of the as-fabricated PNCQDs were explored and the optical spectra were displayed in Fig. 2 . The UV-vis absorption of PNCQDs was shown in Fig. 2A (blank line). It can be seen that PNCQDs possess two absorption bands with one located at 275 nm which is attributed to the n→π* transition of the CO band, and the other one centered at 336 nm which is likely originates from the excited defect surface states induced by N and P heteroatoms [41]. The red and blue lines in Fig. 2A are the PL spectra of PNCQDs. It indicates that the PNCQDs exhibit the λ ex and λ em at 403 nm and 502 nm, respectively. As shown in the inset of Fig. 2A, the photograph of PNCQDs dispersion under 365 nm UV light emits strong green fluorescence. To further probe the optical properties of the as-fabricated PNCQDs, the λex-dependent PL behavior, which is mutual in fluorescent carbon materials [19,41], was investigated and illustrated in Fig. 2B. This λex-dependent PL behavior is attributed to the surface state which affects the band gap of PNCQDs [37,41]. The surface state is similar to the molecular state while the size impact results in quantum dimensions, both of which are attributed to the complexity of the excited states of PNCQDs [42]. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S1, the Φ
s of the as-fabricated PNCQDs was calculated to be 9.6%. The photostability of the fabricated PNCQDs was investigated by extended detecting of fluorescence intensity at 502 nm. The results (Fig. S8) indicate the inappreciable variation of fluorescence intensity of PNCQDs under UV irradiation (365 nm) about 5 h.

One extraordinary important finding is that the as-fabricated PNCQDs could be used as a highly sensitive Cr(VI) nanoprobe. To achieve high detection efficiency, the pH effect on the PNCQDs fluorescence was surveyed at first. These responses were severally measured three times at each pH, and the average values were calculated. Fig. S9A shows that the negligible changes of fluorescent intensity of PNCQDs were observed in the pH range of 3.0–10.0, indicating that this pH range has no obvious effect on the fluorescence intensity of PNCQDs. Fig. S9B depicted the fluorescence intensity of PNCQDs in the presence of Cr(VI) at various pHs (3.0–12). Again, the fluorescence intensity does not vary too much in the pH range of 3.0–10. Herein, when the pHs of stock solutions of Cr(VI) are in the range of 5.0–6.0, HCrO4 − and CrO4 2− coexist [43]. Considering the normal physiological pH values and the stability of Cr(VI), ultrapure water was chosen for all the subsequent detection assays.

Fig. 3 A depicted the changes in fluorescence of PNCQDs in the presence of different contents of Cr(VI). Meanwhile, the photographs of PNCQDs solution under a UV lamp were taken (bottom right inset of Fig. 3A). It can be seen that the fluorescence color of PNCQDs solution changes from green to colorless as the Cr(VI) concentration increases from 0.0 to 150 μmol/L. The top right inset of Fig. 3A shown the Stern-Volmer (SV) plot of the changes of fluorescent intensities of PNCQDs with increasing Cr(VI) concentration (F
0 /F against Cr(VI) concentration), where F
0 and F are the PL intensities of PNCQDs at λ ex/λ em of 403/502 nm without and with Cr(VI), respectively. The quenching efficiency is fitted by SV equation (2):
(2)
F 0
/ F
=
1
+
K
S V
[ Q ]
where K
SV is the SV quenching constant and [Q] is the concentration of Cr(VI). The K
SV is calculated to be 1.15 × 105 L/mol with a correlation coefficient R
2 of 0.9936. The F
0 /F curve is linearly dependent on Cr(VI) concentration in the range of 1.5–30 μmol/L (i.e., 78–1560 μg/L of Cr(VI)), demonstrating the excellent probing property of PNCQDs in the determination of trace Cr(VI). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by taking the PL intensity equal to 3 times the standard deviation of the intensity at the blank (n = 12) divided by K
SV , and the limit of quantification (LOQ) was defined as three times of LOD. The LOD and LOQ of the as-constructed probe were calculated as 23 (1.2 μg/L) and 69 nmol/L at the signal-to-noise of 3 and 10 [41], respectively. The World Health Organization (WHO) formulates that the concentration of Cr(VI) lower than 50 μg/L is acceptable in potable water [3], implying that the proposed fluorescent probe based on PNCQDs is propitious to determining the concentration of Cr(VI) in potable water.

IFE phenomenon of fluorescence usually occurs between the absorber and fluorophore arising from the absorption of the excitation and/or emission light by absorbers in the detection system. A highly effective IFE requires the adequately spectral overlap of the absorption band of an absorber with the excitation and/or emission bands of a fluorophore. Consequently, it is significant to choose an appropriate absorber and fluorophore pair in the IFE-based fluorescence nanoprobe [44]. As shown in Fig. 3B, Cr(VI) depicts three wide absorption peaks at 260, 350 and 450 nm, respectively. Our as-fabricated PNCQDs was chosen as the fluorophore because both the excitation (403 nm) spectra and the emission (502 nm) spectra of PNCQDs revealed precise overlap with the absorption peaks of Cr(VI). As a consequence, Cr(VI) shield excitation and emission light from PNCQDs. Therefore, the fluorescence of PNCQDs could be successfully quenched by the absorbance enhancement of Cr(VI), which assures that the IFE occurs in a highly efficient way. Moreover, in order to understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism between Cr(VI) absorber and PNCQDs fluorophore, the lifetimes of PNCQDs without and with Cr(VI) were measured by TCSPC and the results were displayed in Fig. 3C. The relevant data were summed up in Table S3. The fluorescent decay curves can be well-fitted by a double-exponential function (3):
(3)
I
( t )
=
A 1
exp
( − t /
τ 1
)
+
A 2
exp
( − t /
τ 2
)
where τ
1 and τ
2 are the time constants of the two radiative decay channels; A
1 and A
2 are the corresponding amplitudes. According to the best fitting data, the τ
1 (A
1 ) of PNCQDs and PNCQDs/Cr(VI) are originated to be 3.49 ns (46.99%) and 3.37 ns (44.78%), respectively; the τ
2 (A
2 ) of PNCQDs and PNCQDs/Cr(VI) are originated to be 10.16 ns (53.01%) and 9.96 ns (55.22%), respectively. The average lifetime values of PNCQDs and PNCQDs/Cr(VI) are 7.03 and 7.01 ns, respectively. Almost no changes of lifetime indicates that there is no significant electron-transfer process between PNCQDs and Cr(VI), illustrating that the fluorescence quenching of PNCQDs by Cr(VI) is attributed to IFE rather than other possible mechanisms. Furthermore, the selectivity, sensitivity and interferences are significant arguments to assess the capability of the probing system. The effects of representative metal ions (K+, Na+, Ag+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+ and Cr(VI)) and anion ions (F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, NO2 −, H2PO4 −, SCN−, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, S2O3 2−, HPO4 2−, SO3 2−, C2O4 2− and Cr2O7 2−) on PNCQDs fluorescence quenching under the same conditions were investigated and the results were exhibited in Fig. S10. As shown in Fig. S10A, the metal ions and anion ions at the same concentration of 0.5 mmol/L do not cause the slight fluorescence changes (defined as the relative change of fluorescence intensity in 66–117% as compared to blank [41]), indicating that all the test ions at the same concentration level as that of Cr(VI) do not quench the fluorescence of PNCQDs. In addition, it was found that, apart from Cr(VI) at a concentration of 0.2 mmol/L, the most of metal ions and anion ions at ultrahigh concentrations (20 mmol/L) do not have significant effect on fluorescence intensity of PNCQDs, indicating that the above mentioned metal ions and anion ions do not induce any interference to Cr(VI) determination (Fig. S10B). The PNCQDs-based “turn-off” fluorescent probes possess distinct superiorities of simplicity, sensitivity, and fast response, and hence, it could be potentially applied for Cr(VI) determination in drinking water and environmental water.

Reduction of Cr(VI) in the present of AA have obtained vast of consideration since that AA plays an important role in wiping off Cr(VI). Moreover, the reaction of Cr(VI) with AA could be performed under moderate circumstances (normal temperature, in acidic or neutral environment) [45,46]. In this work, the effects of reducing agents (Na2S, NaNO2, Na2SO3, KI, KBr, Na2S2O3, glucose, GSH, Glu, Gly, CA and AA) on the PNCQDs/Cr(VI) fluorescence recovery under the same conditions were researched and depicted in Fig. S11. It was found that among all the reducing agents examined, AA is the only one that can restore the fluorescence of PNCQDs/Cr(VI), indicating that the “off-on” detection is specific to AA, which is likely due to the following reasons. First, the restored fluorescence in the presence of AA may drive from high-efficiency chelation effect of the functional groups in AA to remove Cr(VI) from the surface of the PNCQDs/Cr(VI) nanoprobe. Second, for PNCQDs/Cr(VI) system, Cr(VI) may be stably reduced to Cr(III)/Cr(0) by AA, leading to eliminating IFE effect and recovering the fluorescence of PNCQDs. Consequently, this PNCQDs/Cr(VI) system could be utilized as a “turn-on” fluorescence probe for AA determination. Fig. 4 shows that the fluorescence of PNCQDs/Cr(VI)/AA mixture is strengthened gradually by enhancing AA concentration. The good linear relationship of ΔF (equation (4)) and AA concentration is observed from 5.0 to 200 μmol/L (top right inset of Fig. 4) with the linear equation of (5),
(4)
Δ F
=
F − F 0
(5)
Δ F
=
1 . 5554
[ AA ]
+
13 . 2799
where F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of PNCQDs/Cr(VI) at λ ex/λ em of 403/502 nm without and with AA, respectively. The LOD of the proposed probe was calculated to be 1.35 μmol/L at the signal-to-noise of 3. This PNCQDs/Cr(VI)-based “turn-on” fluorescence nanoprobe exhibits distinct superiorities of simplicity, sensitivity, and fast response. Fig. S12 clearly reveals that three cycles of the “on-off-on” nanoprobe are possible for Cr(VI) and AA detection and the fluorescence intensity recovery about 70% of original fluorescence in every cycle.

Since PNCQDs could be potentially applied in bioimaging and nanoscale dimensions, the cytotoxicity of PNCQDs should be concerned. To access the cytotoxicity of PNCQDs, the MTT method was used to measure the viability of SiHa cells treated with PNCQDs. SiHa cells were cultured with concentration of PNCQDs at 0, 50, 100, 200, 400, and 800 μg/mL for 48 h. As shown by Fig. 5 , the viability of SiHa cells remains higher than 90% even they were incubated with ultrahigh PNCQDs concentration at 800 μg/mL for 48 h, indicating the naturally low cytotoxicity of PNCQDs.

In order to search for the potential application of PNCQDs for in vitro cellular imaging, the SiHa cells were incubated with PNCQDs aqueous solution (pH = 7.4) for 2 h. As shown in Fig. 6 A(i), the cellular uptakes of PNCQDs were viewed via laser scanning confocal microscopic (LSCM). PNCQDs were internalized and well-decentralized into the cytoplasm region and a few parts of PNCQDs entered into cell nucleus, and cells stained with PNCQDs emit most intense blue, green, and red emissions. When increasing concentrations of Cr2O7 2− entered into SiHa cells nutrient solutions, the blue, green and red emissions were gradually weakened and finally can hardly be sought as displayed in Fig. 6A(ii) and S13B. However, when increasing concentrations of AA was entered into SiHa cells nutrient solutions, the blue, green and red emissions were progressively recovered as depicted in Fig. 6A(iii) and S13C, indicating that emission of PNCQDs/Cr2O7 2− in the cells can be recovered by AA. The LSCM images of SiHa cells cultured with (i) PNCQDs, (ii) PNCQDs/Cr(VI) and (iii) PNCQDs/Cr(VI)/AA (Fig. 6A) possess normal cell morphology, implying that PNCQDs, PNCQDs/Cr(VI) and PNCQDs/Cr(VI)/AA are biocompatible and have minimum cytotoxicity to cells. These cells emitted green (first panels in Fig. 6A) and red (second panels in Fig. 6A) emissions when they were stimulated by 488 nm and 543 nm lasers, respectively. The merged images (the third panels in Fig. 6A) of first and second panels illustrate the excellent ability of PNCQDs in permeating cell membrane. These observations demonstrate that PNCQDs could be utilized as in vitro cellular imaging agent. These cells cultured with PNCQDs and PNCQDs/Cr(VI)/AA emitted stronger fluorescence than those of PNCQDs/Cr(VI), which is due to the fact that Cr(VI) and AA could quench and recover emission of PNCQDs in aqueous solution (vide supra). Fig. 6B depicted the change of intracellular fluorescence intensities of PNCQDs without and with Cr2O7 2− and AA, respectively. The intensities of 21 cells were measured. The λ ex/λ em are 488/500 ± 25 nm. It is prominent that the emission intensities of the cells are in the order of PNCQDs > PNCQDs/Cr(VI)/AA > PNCQDs/Cr(VI). Fig. S13 showed the λex-dependent fluorescence of the PNCQDs, PNCQDs/Cr(VI) and PNCQDs/Cr(VI)/AA-stained cells. The cells display blue, green and red emissions at λ ex of 405, 488 and 543 nm, respectively. Again, the emission intensities of the cells follow the order of PNCQDs > PNCQDs/Cr(VI)/AA > PNCQDs/Cr(VI). The above results illustrate that PNCQDs could serve as a highly efficient fluorescent probe for “on-off-on” determination of Cr(VI) and AA in living cells.

In this work, the phosphorus and nitrogen dual-doped CQDs with fluorescent quantum yield about 9.6% have been fabricated by a rapid, convenient and “green” route with glucose, H3PO4 and EDA as the precursors. The morphologies, chemical structures and optical properties have been extensively investigated by TEM, AFM, DLS, XPS, FTIR, UV-vis absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopy. The fundamental of a simple, efficient, and economical “on-off-on” fluorescence technique for determination of Cr(VI) and AA utilizing PNCQDs as a fluorescence nanoprobe based on IFE have been illustrated. Comparing to classical fluorescence nanoprobes/nanosensors, the as-constructed PNCQDs-based nanoprobes possess better selectivity and higher sensitivity, and could be successfully applied for Cr(VI) detection in drinking water and environmental water. Furthermore, PNCQDs could act as a reagent with the capability for the determination of Cr(VI) and AA in biosystem. Notably, the as-constructed fluorescent probing system has been successfully used for the analysis of AA content in fresh fruits and in commercial fruit juices. The results were proved to be satisfactory, and the data obtained were convincing, which fully indicated that this proposed method was capable for Cr(VI) detection in real samples. In consideration of its simplicity and fast speed, it is promising that PNCQDs could be potentially applied in a wide range of applications, such as water quality monitoring, food quality supervision, and biomedical controlling.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports from the National Science Foundation of China (21575084) and Postgraduate student education innovation project (NO. 2016BY005) are gratefully acknowledged.
